ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mexican Bhel. FOODON_03315091 (snack food, mixed) is the closest defensible parent category for this fusion chaat-style snack.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Mexican Bhel specifically. A0CKP (mixed dishes, grain-based) is the closest defensible parent given the puffed rice / tortilla chip base with vegetable and legume components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept exists for Mexican Bhel or analogous fusion chaat dishes.

allergens: gluten (sev, tortilla chips may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Mexican Bhel is approximately 250 g, typically presented as a full plate or bowl at a restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Bhel Puri (Indian chaat snack) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central component data for Mexican elements (black beans, corn, avocado, lime juice)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Mexican Bhel is a fusion dish not directly listed in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for traditional Bhel Puri (puffed rice, sev, chutneys, vegetables) with USDA FoodData Central data for Mexican-origin components (black beans, corn, avocado). The blend is weighted to reflect a typical restaurant preparation: approximately 60% traditional bhel base and 40% Mexican additions. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant values may vary.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Bhel Puri and mixed grain-legume snack foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mexican Bhel. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Bhel Puri (puffed rice-based chaat, GI approximately 50–55, low category) as the closest analogous food. The Mexican additions (black beans GI ~30, corn GI ~52, avocado GI ~15) are expected to maintain or slightly lower the overall GI relative to plain Bhel Puri. A blended GI of 52 is used as a defensible central estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 61.25) / 100 ≈ 31.9 (using 250 g serving × 24.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 61.25 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from chaat masala, tamarind chutney, and sev
hypertension.guidance: Mexican Bhel contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (775 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt chutneys are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from puffed rice and tortilla chip base, partially offset by high dietary fibre from legumes
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 and a glycemic load of about 32 per standard serving, Mexican Bhel poses a moderate glycaemic challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; consuming a smaller portion (100–150 g) and pairing with additional legumes or vegetables can help blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and plant-based ingredients including avocado and black beans
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mexican Bhel is predominantly plant-based with low saturated fat (0.9 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful dietary fibre (4.1 g per 100 g) from legumes and vegetables, making it a relatively heart-friendly option when sodium intake is managed through portion control.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mexican Bhel is a contemporary Indo-Mexican fusion snack that reimagines the classic Indian street food Bhel Puri by incorporating Mexican culinary elements. Traditional Bhel Puri consists of puffed rice (murmura), sev (fried chickpea-flour noodles), chopped onion, tomato, green chilli, coriander, tamarind chutney, and chaat masala. In the Mexican Bhel variant, these elements are supplemented or partially replaced with black beans, sweet corn, diced avocado or guacamole, jalapeño, and lime juice, while tortilla chips may substitute for or complement the puffed rice base. The dish is assembled cold and served immediately to preserve the crunch of the puffed rice or chips. The flavour profile is tangy, spicy, and slightly sweet, with the tamarind chutney providing sourness, chaat masala delivering a pungent umami-spice note, and the avocado contributing creaminess. Texturally, the dish contrasts crispy sev and puffed rice against soft beans, juicy tomato, and creamy avocado. Nutritionally, Mexican Bhel is a moderate-energy, high-fibre, low-saturated-fat dish that derives protein primarily from legumes. It is naturally vegan and dairy-free. The dish is relevant in clinical nutrition discussions for its moderate glycaemic index (~52), meaningful dietary fibre content (~4.1 g per 100 g), and moderate sodium load from chutneys and seasoning. It is popular in Indian fusion restaurants in Singapore and internationally as a lighter, plant-forward starter or snack.

confidence_reason: Mexican Bhel is a fusion dish not directly listed in any approved primary nutritional database. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for traditional Bhel Puri with USDA FoodData Central data for Mexican components. The GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (Bhel Puri) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and transparent, but actual restaurant-specific values may differ.
notes: 1. Mexican Bhel is a fusion dish served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine restaurants in Singapore (field survey listing). The cuisine tag in the input JSON was 'Turkish', which is inconsistent with the dish itself; the dish is Indian fusion and has been reclassified accordingly. The restaurant context (Turkish/Lebanese cuisine restaurant) suggests this may be a cross-cuisine menu item. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Bhel Puri data and USDA FoodData Central component data; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by composition. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as no animal or fortified ingredients are present; confirmed zero by composition. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero by composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the dish is fully plant-based; naturally zero. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and all micronutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for the constituent ingredients and are approximate. 5. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the dish is served in Singapore, even though the dish itself is Indian fusion. The Chinese name 墨西哥印度脆饼沙拉 is a descriptive translation meaning 'Mexican Indian crispy cracker salad'. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on Bhel Puri from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for Mexican Bhel exists in approved sources. 7. Sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4), potassium_mg (280.0), calcium_mg (42.0), iron_mg (1.8), vitamin_c_mg (8.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.12), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.07), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.15), folate_mcg_dfe (38.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (110.0), magnesium_mg (38.0), zinc_mg (0.8), selenium_mcg (4.2), water_g (58.0) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not -1 sentinel values; they are positive estimates. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as component-level estimates were available for all fields.
